objectives: Describe how magnetic, flash/solid-state and optical storage work and compare their pros and cons
Explain the differences between RAM and ROM and how virtual memory and virtual/cloud storage work
Apply device characteristics to justify suitable input, output and storage choices for a given scenario

retrieval.q: How does a magnetic hard disk drive (HDD) store data?
retrieval.a: By magnetising patterns/polarity on a spinning platter, read and written by a read/write head.

retrieval.q: Which storage type uses a laser to detect pits and lands?
State two differences between RAM and ROM.
retrieval.a: RAM is volatile and read/write holding running programs; ROM is non-volatile and normally read-only holding bootstrap/BIOS firmware.

retrieval.q: From 1.1.1, what is cache memory and how does it differ from RAM?
retrieval.a: Small very fast memory on/near the CPU holding frequently used data; it is separate from and faster than main RAM.

retrieval.q: From 1.1.2, name one GPGPU use of a GPU.
retrieval.a: Machine learning/neural network training (or simulation, image processing, crypto hashing).

keywords.def: Stores data as magnetised patterns on a spinning platter or tape; huge capacity and low cost per GB but slow and fragile.
EEPROM-based storage that traps electrons in cells with no moving parts; fast, silent, durable but costlier per GB with finite write cycles.
Uses a laser to detect pits and lands on a disc; cheap and portable but low capacity and slow.
Loses its contents when power is removed, e.g. RAM.
Read Only Memory; non-volatile, normally read-only, typically holding the bootstrap loader/BIOS firmware.
Use of secondary storage (a swap/paging file) as extra RAM when physical RAM is full, moving pages in and out.
Excessive paging where the system spends most of its time swapping pages rather than doing useful work, slowing it dramatically.
Remote storage on networked servers, accessible anywhere, scalable and off-site, but needing internet with ongoing cost and security concerns.

content.heading: Three storage technologies
points: Magnetic (HDD, tape): magnetised patterns on a platter/tape; huge capacity, low cost per GB, but slow, fragile and noisy
Flash/SSD (SSD, USB, SD): traps electrons in EEPROM cells with no moving parts; fast, silent, durable, portable, but costlier per GB with finite write cycles
Optical (CD/DVD/Blu-ray): laser detects pits and lands; very cheap and portable with long shelf life, but low capacity and slow

content.heading: Access methods and secondary storage
points: Secondary storage is non-volatile, retaining data without power
Tape is serial access, ideal for cheap high-capacity archive/backup but poor for random access
HDD and SSD offer direct/random access; flash uses wear levelling to spread writes across cells

content.heading: RAM vs ROM
points: RAM is volatile (lost on power off), read and written, and holds running programs and data
ROM is non-volatile, normally read-only, and holds the bootstrap/BIOS firmware
Both are primary memory, distinct from slower secondary storage

content.heading: Virtual memory and cloud storage
points: Virtual memory: when RAM is full the OS moves less-used pages to a swap/paging file on disk and back as needed, letting larger programs run
Drawback: disk is far slower than RAM, so heavy paging causes disk thrashing
Virtual/cloud storage: remote networked servers giving access anywhere, scalable capacity and off-site backup, but needing internet with ongoing cost and security concerns

worked_example.title: Justifying a device choice against a scenario
steps: Read the requirements carefully: portable storage for large video files, frequently rewritten, fast transfer, durable
Match each requirement to a characteristic rather than listing generic pros
Choose flash/SSD (external SSD or USB flash drive) as the recommendation
Justify: no moving parts gives durability and portability; high transfer speeds suit large video files; easily rewritable for frequent changes
Compare against an alternative (e.g. an HDD is cheaper per GB but slower and fragile when carried), then state the conclusion linked to the scenario

task: Give students three short scenarios (e.g. high-volume office printing, occasional home photo printing, long-term cheap archive). For each, recommend a device/storage type and justify it by linking specific characteristics to the stated requirements.
challenge: Write a five-mark style answer recommending a printer for both a busy office and an occasional home photo user, using comparison language (whereas) so every characteristic is tied to a requirement and a justified judgement is reached.

exit_ticket.q: Why is flash/SSD storage more durable than a magnetic HDD?
exit_ticket.a: It has no moving parts, so it is shock-resistant as well as faster, silent and lower power.

exit_ticket.q: What does volatile mean, and which of RAM/ROM is volatile?
exit_ticket.a: Volatile means contents are lost when power is removed; RAM is volatile, ROM is non-volatile.
When excessive paging means the system spends most of its time swapping pages in and out of disk rather than doing useful work.
